beats.text: k-means optimizes one objective, the within-cluster sum of squared distances, and that single choice bakes in three silent assumptions.
The inertia objective sums squared Euclidean distances from each point to its assigned centre, so distance-to-a-centre is the only shape information available.

visual.note: Squared distance to a single centre is all k-means knows

beats.text: From that formula fall three consequences: clusters are implicitly spherical, equal-sized, and exhaustive, so every point must join some cluster.
Each assumption has a counterexample: interleaving crescents, stretched ellipses, and a few outliers that drag a centroid across the plane.
There is no universally correct clustering, so each method optimizes a proxy and the right method is the one whose proxy matches your purpose.
Agglomerative hierarchical clustering refuses to commit to k, building the whole family of clusterings from n singletons up to one cluster.

visual.sub: Merge the two closest clusters, repeat

beats.text: Everything turns on the linkage: single uses the nearest pair, complete uses the farthest pair, and Ward merges the pair that raises within-cluster error least.

visual.note: Single, complete, and Ward give genuinely different trees

beats.text: Each merge reduces the cluster count by exactly one, so n points always produce exactly n minus one merges and that many internal nodes.
A dendrogram draws every merge at a height equal to its linkage distance, and cutting at a height h reads off a flat partition.
A cut at height one point zero keeps the merges below it and undoes the three above it, leaving four clusters from six leaves.

visual.label: clusters from a cut at h = 1.0

beats.text: The naive algorithm is order n cubed time and holds the full distance matrix, and its merges are greedy and irrevocable.
DBSCAN treats a cluster as a connected region of high point density of any shape, surrounded by regions of low density.

visual.sub: Density, not distance to a centre

beats.text: A point is a core point when its epsilon-ball holds at least minPts neighbours, including itself, marking it as sitting in a crowd.

visual.note: N_epsilon(p) is the set of points within radius epsilon

beats.text: Three point types follow: core points in a crowd, border points on the fringe, and noise points that belong to no cluster.
The noise label is the quiet superpower, since every other method here forces every point into some cluster while DBSCAN refuses.

visual.xlabel: dense crowd with isolated noise at the edges

beats.text: minPts is roughly twice the dimensionality, so about four for two-dimensional data, and epsilon is read from the k-distance elbow.
One global epsilon struggles when densities differ, so HDBSCAN removes the knob and is the default density clusterer today.

visual.note: A single global radius is the main DBSCAN limitation

beats.text: A Gaussian mixture model softens both of k-means' hard commitments by modelling data as k blended Gaussians with soft membership.
The density of any point is the weighted sum over all components, with mixing weights that are non-negative and sum to one.

visual.note: A full covariance frees the model from k-means' circles

beats.text: EM alternates an E-step that computes every responsibility and an M-step that re-estimates weights, means, and covariances.

visual.note: E-step: a soft assignment, M-step: a soft update

beats.text: Each full iteration increases the data log-likelihood or leaves it unchanged, climbing monotonically toward a local maximum.
Counting only means, a two-dimensional model with five components has two numbers per mean, so ten mean parameters in total.
k-means is the limiting case of a GMM with equal weights, shared spherical covariance, and variance driven to zero.
Without labels there is no accuracy to optimize, so validation splits into internal geometric checks and the better external downstream answer.

visual.sub: Internal checks vs the downstream task

beats.text: The elbow method plots inertia against k, but inertia falls monotonically all the way to zero, so often there is no clean elbow.
The silhouette compares cohesion within a point's own cluster against separation from the nearest rival, landing between minus one and one.

visual.note: a is cohesion, b is separation from the nearest other cluster

beats.text: The Bayesian Information Criterion subtracts a penalty for every parameter, trading fit against parsimony, and lower is better.

visual.note: The m ln(n) term punishes every extra parameter

beats.text: Silhouette and inertia favour convex blobs, so they will rank a wrong k-means clustering above a correct DBSCAN one.
Every algorithm returns clusters even on pure noise, so before trusting a partition ask whether structure exists at all.

visual.label: Adjusted Rand Index for a random clustering
